erroneous_event: He invites Eanna along, expecting his blessing and perhaps help convincing their mother to prepare a feast—after he succeeds. <error>Eanna startles him with a flat refusal: “Not to the swan’s nest tucked among the rushes, Columban; I won’t help you steal a bride from there—Fionnualla or any other.”</error>
story:
Eanna tries to stop his older brother Columban from running off to “pick his bride” by magic. Columban, a celebrated fighter now expected to marry for the clan’s good, mocks the women proposed to him. Eanna, who knows Muirgel Mac Airt’s skill at the loom and Bebinn O’Brien’s gift with accounts, finds them excellent matches and winces at Columban’s scorn. But Columban, proud of his dabbling in sorcery and their ancestor’s old selkie tale, insists: tonight’s full moon, a long walk to a lough, and the one ritual that can make him a hero’s equal bride. He invites Eanna along, expecting his blessing and perhaps help convincing their mother to prepare a feast—after he succeeds. Eanna startles him with a flat refusal: “Not to the swan’s nest tucked among the rushes, Columban; I won’t help you steal a bride from there—Fionnualla or any other.”

They set out with a pack donkey, a cauldron, sacred herbs, holy water, silver knife and chain, and Eanna’s one practical afterthought: a spare kirtle for a transformed lady. At the shore, Columban refuses to work in secret at home; the spell needs a final ingredient gathered on site. He flashes a white feather. He means to free—and claim—a Swan Maiden. He has already named her Fionnualla and dreams aloud of grace, moon-pale skin, and courtly admiration while the herbs scent the air and the moon climbs.

Under the silver light, he binds the feather to the knife with the chain and drops all three into the cauldron. A clean white flare; the fire dies. Unburnt, he draws out blade and chain now glowing with inner light, the feather consumed. Entranced, he prowls the shoreline until the charm tugs toward a swan’s nest tucked among rushes. The bird gleams like a second moon, head pillowed under wing. Silent as frost, Columban touches the knife to her breast and whispers, “Swan Maiden, I, Columban O’Quin, free you and claim you as my bride,” and makes the cut.

For an instant the brothers glimpse double: swan to woman, woman to swan, a wrongness that turns the stomach. Light engulfs the nest and settles around a white-feathered cloak and a curled human form. When she unfolds, she keeps her head down. A rank odor seeps into the night. She rises—and rises. The face is astonishingly lovely; the body beneath is colossal, shoulders broad as lintels, arms like a sailor’s thighs, legs corded and mud-stained, strength poured into every plane. Eanna feels the truth: something in Columban’s plan was never right.

Columban rallies. Chain in hand, he commands, “I name you Fionnualla. I have given you human form. Bow to me.” She answers with a contemptuous hiss and an outrageous blast, then fixes him with dark eyes. “I am your master,” he tries again, stepping close.

“SCQREEKONK!”

She moves like a nightmare sprung. One massive fist crushes him to the ground, then both hands and boots batter him senseless. She flings knife and chain into the lough, snatches his ankle, bites his calf to still his kicking, and hoists him upside down to march for the water. His pleas tumble out in bubbles and mud. At the bank, she stands in moonlit majesty and throws him, a sack of pride and bruises, into the lake.

Eanna approaches her with respect and caution. “Lady… Goddess of the wild, if I may?” He finds the tiny clasp, wraps the feathered cloak about her shoulders, fastens it with his small, steady hands. Light swallows her; a filthy, furious swan remains. She glides off, pauses only to bite Columban’s nose and buffet his head with both wings, then disappears.

Eanna wades out to his brother, battered and floating like the dead. One eye is swollen shut; his torn nose and missing tooth make him look younger and foolish. “Perhaps,” Columban whispers as Eanna hauls him to shore, “I judged my matches too quickly. Bebinn is a very gentle lady.” A beat. “Eanna? I don’t think magic really solves problems like this.”

“Yes, brother,” Eanna says, and means it.
